A natural variable reference confusion — both left and right are in scope with .shape[0] attributes. Only asymmetric compounds (e.g., rot & (sh1&sh2)) reveal the bug, making it a cross-function semantic mutation that is hard to catch without testing non-symmetric nested models.

diff --git a/astropy/modeling/separable.py b/astropy/modeling/separable.py
index 45bea36085..5063d2df58 100644
--- a/astropy/modeling/separable.py
+++ b/astropy/modeling/separable.py
@@ -59,7 +59,7 @@ def is_separable(transform):

     is_separable = separable_matrix.sum(1)
-    is_separable = np.where(is_separable != 1, False, True)
+    is_separable = np.where(is_separable < 1, False, True)
     return is_separable
mutation.explanation: CHOSEN: E1; Changes the separability criterion from row_sum != 1 to row_sum < 1 in is_separable(). Redefines separable as "fewer than one input dependency" instead of "exactly one". For outputs depending on multiple inputs (row_sum > 1, e.g., Rotation2D outputs), the condition row_sum < 1 is False so np.where(False, False, True) = True, incorrectly marking them as separable. Hard to detect because < 1 and != 1 differ only for values > 1, which only appear in non-separable compound models.

CHOSEN: A1; For nested inner classes, the serializer correctly computes the qualified name but adds a spurious 'from module import OuterClass' import entry alongside the correct 'import module'. The name string is exactly right, so shallow tests that only inspect the class path pass; only tests that also assert on the import set (like test_serialize_nested_class) detect the bug. Looks like a developer reasonably trying to 'help' callers by pre-importing the outer class, but misunderstanding that class names cannot be imported as modules.

Sits at the central branching node of TypeSerializer; any test using a non-builtin type (which is nearly all migration tests) will fail, but the logical inversion is easy to miss at a glance.

CHOSEN: C1; Adds a branch that routes all enum.Enum subclasses (including TextChoices and IntegerChoices) back to the old __name__-based path, while non-Enum types correctly use __qualname__. Plausible because EnumSerializer already handles Enum *instances* specially, so a developer might assume Enum *classes* also need separate treatment in TypeSerializer. Passes all non-Enum type serialization tests; only fails when serializing an inner Enum class like WriterTests.NestedChoices.

CHOSEN: D1; Detects Enum classes via the internal _value2member_map_ attribute (a dict built by EnumMeta.__new__ during class initialization) and falls back to __name__ for them. This attribute is an implementation detail of Python's enum machinery — not part of the public API — making the detection non-obvious. The condition reads like duck-typing ('does this class have an enum member map?'), which is a natural pattern in Django code, but breaks inner Enum class serialization in the same way as the original bug.

CHOSEN: E1; For inner classes, replaces dots in __qualname__ with underscores, yielding paths like 'migrations.test_writer.WriterTests_NestedChoices' instead of the correct 'migrations.test_writer.WriterTests.NestedChoices'. The import is still correct, the result is syntactically valid Python, and top-level class serialization is unaffected. Invalidates the exact string assertion in test_serialize_nested_class; the generated migration also silently refers to a nonexistent attribute, failing only at migration execution time without a syntax error.

CHOSEN: A1; Adds self.attrs['checked'] = True inside CheckboxInput.get_context alongside the new-dict creation. After the first True-value render, the widget instance's self.attrs is permanently contaminated with checked:True. SplitArrayWidget reuses the same widget instance in its loop, so subsequent False-value subwidgets inherit checked via build_attrs(self.attrs={'checked':True}, extra_attrs={}). Hard to detect: the extra line looks like a developer tracking widget state alongside the return value.

CHOSEN: B1; Adds 'if attrs: attrs[checked]=True' guard before the new-dict creation in CheckboxInput.get_context. When attrs is a non-empty dict (truthy), it is mutated in-place before the new dict is created. test_get_context_does_not_mutate_attrs passes attrs={'checked':False} which is truthy, so the original dict gets checked:True written before the guard even creates a new dict. Hard to detect: looks like an optimization 'only touch existing attrs dict when it's already there'.

CHOSEN: C1; Replaces the single-expression new-dict creation with a three-step idiom: attrs = attrs or {}; attrs['checked'] = True; attrs = dict(attrs). The 'attrs or {}' on a non-empty dict returns the SAME reference (not a copy), so attrs['checked']=True mutates the caller's dict before dict(attrs) makes the final copy. Both F2P tests fail: test_get_context_does_not_mutate_attrs because the input dict is mutated, test_checkbox_get_context_attrs because final_attrs in SplitArrayWidget gets checked:True. Hard to detect: final dict(attrs) copy creates a false impression of correctness.

CHOSEN: D1; Splits the new-dict creation into a conditional: if 'checked' not in attrs create new dict (safe), else mutate attrs in-place (unsafe). When attrs already carries 'checked' (e.g. {'checked':False} in the F2P test), the else branch directly mutates it, failing test_get_context_does_not_mutate_attrs. Hard to detect: the if-branch is correct; the bug only surfaces when 'checked' is pre-set in the caller's attrs dict, an edge case.

CHOSEN: E1; Modifies Widget.build_attrs to call base_attrs.update(extra_attrs or {}) after computing the correct result. Since build_attrs is called as build_attrs(self.attrs, attrs) in Widget.get_context, self.attrs gets permanently updated with every set of extra_attrs passed in. After CheckboxInput.get_context renders a True-value checkbox (passing {'checked':True} as extra_attrs), self.attrs={'checked':True} persists. SplitArrayWidget's False-value subwidget then gets {'checked':True} from build_attrs({'checked':True}, {}), failing test_checkbox_get_context_attrs. Hard to detect: result is computed correctly first; the update looks like syncing base state.

This is functionally equivalent to not having the method at all (same as the pre-fix behavior via Expression base class). This is the hardest mutation to detect: it passes for annotation-based queries but fails specifically for order_by('?') combined with aggregation.
